back_translation: In the days when the judges ruled, there arose a famine in the land. So a certain man went out from Bethlehem in Judah to reside in the land of Moab, together with his wife and his two sons.

That man's name was Elimelech, his wife's name was Naomi, and his two sons' names were Mahlon and Chilion. They were Ephrathites from Bethlehem in Judah, and they arrived in the land of Moab and settled there.
Then Elimelech, Naomi's husband, died, so she was left with her two sons.
The two sons took Moabite women as wives. One was named Orpah, the other was named Ruth. They lived there about ten years.
Subsequently both Mahlon and Chilion also died. So that woman was left alone, without her two sons and without her husband.

When Naomi and her two daughters-in-law heard while in the land of Moab that the LORD had visited His people, by giving them food, she arose and prepared to return from the land of Moab together with her two daughters-in-law.
So she went out from the place where she had been, together with her two daughters-in-law, and they journeyed back to the land of Judah.
Then Naomi said to her two daughters-in-law, 'Each of you, return to your mother's house. May the LORD show steadfast love to you, just as you have shown it to those who have departed and to me.
May the LORD grant that you find settled-peace, namely in the house of a new husband each.' Then she kissed them goodbye, and they raised their voices and wept.
But they answered her, 'No, we will return with you to your people.'
But Naomi said, 'My daughters, return. Why would you come with me? Do I still have sons in my womb who could become husbands for you?
My daughters, return, for I am too old to have a husband again. Even if I should say, 'I still have hope,' even if this very night I were married, and even if I could bear sons —

Would you wait until they grew up? Would you refrain from marriage in order to wait for them? No, my daughters, for I am much more bitter than you, since the hand of the LORD has stretched out against me.'
They raised their voices and wept again. Orpah kissed her mother-in-law goodbye, but Ruth still held fast and clung closely to her.
Naomi said, 'Look, your other daughter-in-law has returned to her people and to her gods. You also return after her.'

But Ruth answered, 'Do not press me to leave you or to turn back from following you. For wherever you go, I will go, and wherever you lodge, I will lodge. Your people will be my people, and your God will be my God.
Wherever you die, I also will die, and there I will be buried as well. May the LORD punish me, and make it even heavier than that, if anything other than death tears me from you.'
When Naomi saw that Ruth was firmly resolved to go with her, she stopped trying to persuade her.
So the two journeyed on until they came to Bethlehem. When the two arrived at Bethlehem, the whole town was stirred up because of them, and the women said to each other, 'Is this Naomi?'
But she said to them, 'Do not call me Naomi. Call me Mara, for the Almighty has dealt very bitterly with me.

I went out from here full, but the LORD has brought me back empty-handed. Why then call me Naomi, when the LORD has testified against me as a hostile witness, and the Almighty has brought suffering upon me?'
So Naomi returned from the land of Moab, together with Ruth the Moabitess, her daughter-in-law. They arrived in Bethlehem at the beginning of the barley harvest.
